title: Chapter 8: What the Rot King Built
content: Marcus's maps were works of art.

He'd drawn them on the backs of old invoices, on scraps of leather, on a sheet of salvaged metal he'd hammered flat with his own hands. Every detail of the Keep — every corridor, every guard post, every hidden passage — was marked with the obsessive precision of someone who'd been planning an assault for twelve years and never found anyone brave enough to execute it.

"The outer wall is the easiest part," he said, tracing the ring with a calloused finger. "Twenty feet of fused bone and black iron. At night, it's manned by two dozen Barons and a rotating guard of fledglings. During the day..." He looked up at Kael. "During the day, it's manned by nothing. Torpor. Every last one of them."

"They've never been attacked during the day," Sera said. She was leaning against the forge, her glowing veins pulsing faintly in the firelight. "Not once in two hundred years. Why guard against something that's impossible?"

Kael traced the path from the outer wall to the inner sanctum. Three rings of defense, each higher than the last, winding upward toward the throne room at the peak. Torches burned in iron sconces along every corridor — Marcus had marked them on the map with tiny flame symbols. The heat would be oppressive, the air thick with old blood and incense. But at noon, when every guard was in torpor, it would also be empty.

"The throne room is the problem," Marcus continued. His finger stopped at the central chamber — a circular space marked with Dredd's personal sigil, a crown made of bones. "It's sealed. No windows. No natural light sources. Just torches and braziers and the Rot King's own blood-magic keeping the darkness absolute. You could walk right up to his throne, but you couldn't bring the sun in with you."
"Unless the ceiling collapsed," Kael said.

"That's where the structural weakness comes in." Marcus pointed to a section of the throne room marked with red ink — a jagged line that ran from the center of the ceiling to the eastern wall. "Fifty years ago, a cave-in cracked the roof. Dredd had it patched, but the repairs were cheap. Corrupt. The engineer who did the work was executed a year later for an unrelated offense, and no one's inspected the patch since." He met Kael's eyes. "I've run the math. Twelve solid hits to the right support beam, and the entire ceiling comes down. Everything above it — rock, earth, old foundations — crashes into the throne room. And if you time it right..."
"If I time it right, the collapse opens a hole to the surface. Real sunlight floods the chamber. Every vampire in the room burns." Kael traced the line on the map. "Including Dredd."
"Including Dredd."

That night, after Marcus had closed his shop and retreated to whatever bolt-hole he used to survive Greyhaven's darker hours, Kael and Sera stood on an abandoned balcony overlooking the underground city.

The view was dizzying. Torches flickered in the darkness like a scattered constellation, tracing the concentric rings of Greyhaven's architecture. The blood market was still active below — vampires moving through the stalls, humans hurrying home before the late-night hunting hour began. The Keep loomed in the center of it all, a black silhouette against the cavern's false stars.
"You're really going to do this," Sera said. It wasn't a question.

"I have to. Dredd is the gatekeeper. If I kill him, I become a Viscount. His territory, his army, his resources — they become mine. I can start building something real. An army that doesn't hide from the sun. A city that doesn't treat humans like cattle."
"And then?"
"And then the Counts will notice. Orsi, in the east. The others who've been watching Dredd's territory for centuries, waiting for a weakness. They'll come for me the same way Vereck did."
"And you'll kill them too."

"Or make them kneel. I haven't decided which is more useful." He turned to face her. The golden glow in her veins was brighter tonight — he could see it through her sleeves, faint lines of light tracing the paths of her arteries. "You don't have to be here for this. You could go south. Find your father. Tell him you're alive."
"While you fight a Viscount alone?"
"While I fight a Viscount who's unconscious."

"Unconscious vampires still have defenses. Blood wards. Traps. Progeny who might wake if their maker is threatened." She shook her head. "I'm not leaving. I'm your anchor, Kael. Without me, the sun kills you. Without the sun, this whole plan is suicide."
"I could find another anchor. A willing human. Someone who—"

"No." Her voice was sharp enough to cut. "The Pact is ours. I didn't go through three days of venom poisoning and a month of turning into... whatever I'm becoming... just to hand the bond to a stranger. We're in this together. All the way."
Kael was quiet for a moment. Below them, Greyhaven's torches flickered. Above them, the cavern ceiling was lost in darkness.
"Together," he said finally. "All the way."
Sera reached out and took his hand. The contact sent a pulse through the Pact — warmth, light, the strange intimacy of two people who were slowly becoming parts of each other.
"When this is over," she said, "when you're a Viscount and Greyhaven is yours and the Counts are kneeling... promise me something."
"What?"
"If I change too much. If I lose myself. If I stop being human and start being something you can't recognize..." She met his eyes. "Don't let me become a monster. Whatever it takes. Promise me."

Kael looked at her glowing veins, her golden-threaded eyes, the faint luminescence that was spreading through her skin like sunrise through clouds. She was already changing. Already becoming something the world had never seen. But she was still Sera. Still the paladin who'd reached out a bloodstained hand in a ruined church. Still the woman who'd given him the sun.
"I promise," he said.
And somewhere in the Keep, Viscount Dredd stirred in his torpor, troubled by a dream he couldn't quite remember.
